subject: Sonnet 86
source_text: Was it the proud full sail of his great verse, Bound for the prize of (all too precious) you, That did my ripe thoughts in my brain inhearse, Making their tomb the womb wherein they grew? Was it his spirit, by spirits taught to write, Above a mortal pitch, that struck me dead? No, neither he, nor his compeers by night Giving him aid, my verse astonished. He nor that affable familiar ghost Which nightly gulls him with intelligence, As victors of my silence cannot boast, I was not sick of any fear from thence. But when your countenance filled up his line, Then lacked I matter, that enfeebled mine.

subject: Sonnet 87
source_text: Farewell! thou art too dear for my possessing, And like enough thou know’st thy estimate, The charter of thy worth gives thee releasing: My bonds in thee are all determinate. For how do I hold thee but by thy granting, And for that riches where is my deserving? The cause of this fair gift in me is wanting, And so my patent back again is swerving. Thyself thou gav’st, thy own worth then not knowing, Or me to whom thou gav’st it, else mistaking, So thy great gift upon misprision growing, Comes home again, on better judgement making. Thus have I had thee as a dream doth flatter, In sleep a king, but waking no such matter.

subject: Sonnet 107
source_text: Not mine own fears, nor the prophetic soul, Of the wide world, dreaming on things to come, Can yet the lease of my true love control, Supposed as forfeit to a confined doom. The mortal moon hath her eclipse endured, And the sad augurs mock their own presage, Incertainties now crown themselves assured, And peace proclaims olives of endless age. Now with the drops of this most balmy time, My love looks fresh, and death to me subscribes, Since spite of him I’ll live in this poor rhyme, While he insults o’er dull and speechless tribes. And thou in this shalt find thy monument, When tyrants’ crests and tombs of brass are spent.

subject: Sonnet 108
source_text: What’s in the brain that ink may character, Which hath not figured to thee my true spirit, What’s new to speak, what now to register, That may express my love, or thy dear merit? Nothing sweet boy, but yet like prayers divine, I must each day say o’er the very same, Counting no old thing old, thou mine, I thine, Even as when first I hallowed thy fair name. So that eternal love in love’s fresh case, Weighs not the dust and injury of age, Nor gives to necessary wrinkles place, But makes antiquity for aye his page, Finding the first conceit of love there bred, Where time and outward form would show it dead.

If he were living, I would try him yet;— Lend me an arm;—the rest have worn me out With several applications; nature and sickness Debate it at their leisure. Welcome, Count; My son’s no dearer. subject: Countess

subject: Countess's reflection on youth
source_text: Even so it was with me when I was young; If ever we are nature’s, these are ours; this thorn Doth to our rose of youth rightly belong; Our blood to us, this to our blood is born; It is the show and seal of nature’s truth, Where love’s strong passion is impress’d in youth.

subject: SCENE V

subject: The letter
source_text: STEWARD. [_Reads._] _I am Saint Jaques’ pilgrim, thither gone. Ambitious love hath so in me offended That barefoot plod I the cold ground upon, With sainted vow my faults to have amended. Write, write, that from the bloody course of war My dearest master, your dear son, may hie. Bless him at home in peace, whilst I from far His name with zealous fervour sanctify. His taken labours bid him me forgive; I, his despiteful Juno, sent him forth From courtly friends, with camping foes to live, Where death and danger dog the heels of worth. He is too good and fair for death and me; Whom I myself embrace to set him free._

SONG
      Wedding is great Juno’s crown,

            O blessed bond of board and bed. ’Tis Hymen peoples every town,

            High wedlock then be honoured.

subject: Jaques
source_text: JAQUES. Sir, by your patience. If I heard you rightly, The Duke hath put on a religious life And thrown into neglect the pompous court. JAQUES DE BOYS. He hath.

subject: Jaques

subject: Epilogue
source_text:
EPILOGUE

ROSALIND. It is not the fashion to see the lady the epilogue, but it is no more unhandsome than to see the lord the prologue.

subject: Rosalind
source_text: ROSALIND. It is not the fashion to see the lady the epilogue... I charge you, O women, for the love you bear to men, to like as much of this play as please you.

subject: Antipholus of Syracuse striking Dromio of Ephesus
source_text: ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. What, wilt thou flout me thus unto my face, Being forbid? There, take you that, sir knave. DROMIO OF EPHESUS. What mean you, sir? for God’s sake hold your hands. Nay, an you will not, sir, I’ll take my heels. [_Exit Dromio._]

How fondly dost thou reason! DROMIO OF SYRACUSE. Time is a very bankrupt, and owes more than he’s worth to season.

source_text: He’s a very dog to the commonalty.

subject: the unnamed man
source_text: Consider you what services he has done for his country?

subject: what he hath done famously
source_text: I say unto you, what he hath done famously he did it to that end. Though soft-conscienced men can be content to say it was for his country, he did it to please his mother and to be partly proud, which he is, even to the altitude of his virtue.

source_text: MENENIUS. Either you must confess yourselves wondrous malicious Or be accused of folly.

subject: First Citizen
source_text: FIRST CITIZEN. Care for us? True, indeed! They ne’er cared for us yet.

subject: First Citizen
source_text: you slander The helms o’ th’ state, who care for you like fathers, When you curse them as enemies... As you malign our senators for that They are not such as you.

subject: The Tale of the Belly
source_text: I shall tell you A pretty tale. It may be you have heard it, But since it serves my purpose, I will venture To stale’t a little more.

subject: Menenius
source_text: MENENIUS. What then? ’Fore me, this fellow speaks. What then? What then? FIRST CITIZEN. Should by the cormorant belly be restrained,

subject: THE TRAGEDY OF CORIOLANUS
source_text: MENENIUS. The senators of Rome are this good belly, And you the mutinous members.

subject: First Citizen
source_text: FIRST CITIZEN. Should by the cormorant belly be restrained, Who is the sink o’ th’ body—

subject: Menenius
source_text: MENENIUS. I will tell you, If you’ll bestow a small—of what you have little— Patience awhile, you’st hear the belly’s answer.

subject: The citizens' protest
source_text: FIRST CITIZEN. The former agents, if they did complain, What could the belly answer? MENENIUS. I will tell you, If you’ll bestow a small—of what you have little— Patience awhile, you’st hear the belly’s answer.

subject: Menenius's fable of the belly
source_text: MENENIUS. The senators of Rome are this good belly, And you the mutinous members. For examine Their counsels and their cares, digest things rightly Touching the weal o’ th’ common, you shall find No public benefit which you receive But it proceeds or comes from them to you And no way from yourselves.

subject: The citizens' protest
source_text: FIRST CITIZEN. Should by the cormorant belly be restrained... MENENIUS. ...The senators of Rome are this good belly, And you the mutinous members.

subject: The citizens' protest
source_text: MENENIUS. The senators of Rome are this good belly, And you the mutinous members.

subject: FIRST CITIZEN
source_text: For that, being one o’ th’ lowest, basest, poorest, Of this most wise rebellion, thou goest foremost.

subject: MENENIUS
source_text: Thou rascal, that art worst in blood to run, Lead’st first to win some vantage.

subject: Battle
source_text: Rome and her rats are at the point of battle;

subject: Caius Martius
source_text: Thanks.—What’s the matter, you dissentious rogues, That, rubbing the poor itch of your opinion, Make yourselves scabs?

subject: Citizen Protest
source_text: What’s the matter, That in these several places of the city You cry against the noble senate, who, Under the gods, keep you in awe, which else Would feed on one another?

subject: FIRST CITIZEN
source_text: What’s the matter, That in these several places of the city You cry against the noble senate, who, Under the gods, keep you in awe, which else Would feed on one another?

subject: Political Activity
source_text: They’ll sit by th’ fire and presume to know What’s done i’ th’ Capitol, who’s like to rise, Who thrives and who declines; side factions and give out Conjectural marriages, making parties strong And feebling such as stand not in their liking Below their cobbled shoes.

subject: Caius Martius
source_text: And let me use my sword, I’d make a quarry With thousands of these quartered slaves as high As I could pick my lance.

subject: Caius Martius
source_text: And let me use my sword, I’d make a quarry With thousands of these quartered slaves as high As I could pick my lance.

subject: The people's complaining
source_text: They vented their complainings, which being answered And a petition granted them—a strange one, To break the heart of generosity And make bold power look pale

subject: Granting of Tribunes
source_text: It will in time Win upon power and throw forth greater themes For insurrection’s arguing.

subject: The Volsces are in arms
source_text: MESSENGER. The news is, sir, the Volsces are in arms. MARTIUS. I am glad on’t. Then we shall ha’ means to vent Our musty superfluity.

subject: Discussion about the Volsces
source_text: The rabble should have first unroofed the city Ere so prevailed with me.

subject: Caius Martius
source_text: MESSENGER. Where’s Caius Martius? MARTIUS. Here. What’s the matter? MESSENGER. The news is, sir, the Volsces are in arms.

subject: Menenius
source_text: MENENIUS. Nay, these are almost thoroughly persuaded; For though abundantly they lack discretion, Yet are they passing cowardly. But I beseech you, What says the other troop?

subject: Messenger
source_text: Enter a Messenger hastily. MESSENGER. Where’s Caius Martius? MARTIUS. Here. What’s the matter? MESSENGER. The news is, sir, the Volsces are in arms.

subject: Sicinius Velutus
source_text: Enter Sicinius Velutus, Junius Brutus, two Tribunes; Cominius, Titus Lartius with other Senators.

subject: Junius Brutus
source_text: Enter Sicinius Velutus, Junius Brutus, two Tribunes; Cominius, Titus Lartius with other Senators.

subject: Cominius
source_text: Enter Sicinius Velutus, Junius Brutus, two Tribunes; Cominius, Titus Lartius with other Senators. ... COMINIUS. You have fought together.

subject: Titus Lartius
source_text: Enter Sicinius Velutus, Junius Brutus, two Tribunes; Cominius, Titus Lartius with other Senators.

subject: First Senator
source_text: FIRST SENATOR. Martius, ’tis true that you have lately told us: The Volsces are in arms.

subject: Caius Martius
source_text: They have a leader, Tullus Aufidius, that will put you to’t. I sin in envying his nobility, And, were I anything but what I am, I would wish me only he. COMINIUS. You have fought together.

subject: Martius
source_text: Then, worthy Martius, Attend upon Cominius to these wars.

subject: Cominius
source_text: Then, worthy Martius, Attend upon Cominius to these wars.

subject: Titus Lartius
source_text: No, Caius Martius, I’ll lean upon one crutch and fight with th’ other Ere stay behind this business.

subject: Martius
source_text: But I do wonder His insolence can brook to be commanded Under Cominius.

subject: Martius
source_text: Only my wars with him. He is a lion That I am proud to hunt... Titus Lartius, thou Shalt see me once more strike at Tullus’ face.

subject: Titus Lartius
source_text: I’ll lean upon one crutch and fight with th’ other Ere stay behind this business.

subject: Martius
source_text: Your company to th’ Capitol, where I know Our greatest friends attend us.

subject: Cominius
source_text: Your company to th’ Capitol, where I know Our greatest friends attend us.

subject: Titus Lartius
source_text: Your company to th’ Capitol, where I know Our greatest friends attend us.

subject: The war against the Volsces
source_text: The present wars devour him! He is grown Too proud to be so valiant.

subject: Sicinius
source_text: Was ever man so proud as is this Martius?

subject: Brutus
source_text: He is grown Too proud to be so valiant.

source_text: The Volsces have much corn; take these rats thither To gnaw their garners.

subject: Tullus Aufidius
source_text: Enter Tullus Aufidius with Senators of Corioles. FIRST SENATOR. So, your opinion is, Aufidius, That they of Rome are entered in our counsels And know how we proceed.

subject: First Senator
source_text: FIRST SENATOR. So, your opinion is, Aufidius, That they of Rome are entered in our counsels And know how we proceed.

subject: Tullus Aufidius
source_text: I have the letter here. Yes, here it is. [_Reads_.]

subject: Cominius
source_text: Cominius, Martius your old enemy, Who is of Rome worse hated than of you,— And Titus Lartius, a most valiant Roman, These three lead on this preparation Whither ’tis bent.

subject: Martius
source_text: Cominius, Martius your old enemy, Who is of Rome worse hated than of you,— And Titus Lartius, a most valiant Roman, These three lead on this preparation Whither ’tis bent.

subject: Titus Lartius
source_text: Cominius, Martius your old enemy, Who is of Rome worse hated than of you,— And Titus Lartius, a most valiant Roman, These three lead on this preparation Whither ’tis bent.

subject: Martius
source_text: Martius your old enemy, Who is of Rome worse hated than of you,—

subject: Second Senator
source_text: SECOND SENATOR. Noble Aufidius, Take your commission; hie you to your bands.

subject: guarding Corioles
source_text: Let us alone to guard Corioles.

subject: Aufidius
source_text: If we and Caius Martius chance to meet, ’Tis sworn between us we shall ever strike Till one can do no more.

subject: Caius Martius
source_text: If we and Caius Martius chance to meet, ’Tis sworn between us we shall ever strike Till one can do no more.

source_text: I pray you, daughter, sing, or express yourself in a more comfortable sort.

subject: Martius
source_text: To a cruel war I sent him, from whence he returned, his brows bound with oak.

subject: a cruel war
source_text: If my son were my husband, I should freelier rejoice in that absence wherein he won honour than in the embracements of his bed where he would show most love.

subject: Lady Valeria
source_text: Madam, the Lady Valeria is come to visit you.

subject: Coriolanus
source_text: His bloody brow With his mailed hand then wiping, forth he goes

subject: Volumnia
source_text: Away, you fool! It more becomes a man Than gilt his trophy.

subject: Hecuba
source_text: The breasts of Hecuba, When she did suckle Hector, looked not lovelier

subject: Hector
source_text: Than Hector’s forehead when it spit forth blood At Grecian sword, contemning.

subject: Virgilia
source_text: Tell Valeria We are fit to bid her welcome. [...] VIRGILIA. I am glad to see your Ladyship.

subject: Volumnia
source_text: Tell Valeria We are fit to bid her welcome. [...] VALERIA. My ladies both, good day to you. VOLUMNIA. Sweet madam.

subject: Virgilia
source_text: Heavens bless my lord from fell Aufidius!

subject: Coriolanus
source_text: Heavens bless my lord from fell Aufidius! VOLUMNIA. He’ll beat Aufidius’ head below his knee And tread upon his neck.

subject: Virgilia
source_text: What are you sewing here? [...] Come, lay aside your stitchery.

subject: Volumnia
source_text: You are manifest housekeepers. What are you sewing here?

subject: Coriolanus's son
source_text: How does your little son?

subject: Coriolanus's son
source_text: O’ my word, the father’s son!

subject: Coriolanus's son
source_text: I saw him run after a gilded butterfly, and when he caught it, he let it go again, and after it again, and over and over he comes, and up again, catched it again.

subject: Coriolanus's son
source_text: he did so set his teeth and tear it. O, I warrant how he mammocked it!

subject: Virgilia
source_text: Come, lay aside your stitchery.

subject: Coriolanus's son
source_text: He had rather see the swords and hear a drum than look upon his schoolmaster.

subject: Virgilia
source_text: In truth, la, go with me, and I’ll tell you excellent news of your husband.

subject: Martius
source_text: I’ll not over the threshold till my lord return from the wars.

subject: Penelope
source_text: Yet they say all the yarn she spun in Ulysses’ absence did but fill Ithaca full of moths.

subject: Virgilia
source_text: Come, I would your cambric were sensible as your finger, that you might leave pricking it for pity.

subject: Cominius
source_text: Thus it is: the Volsces have an army forth, against whom Cominius the General is gone with one part of our Roman power.

subject: Martius
source_text: Your lord and Titus Lartius are set down before their city Corioles.

source_text: Your lord and Titus Lartius are set down before their city Corioles.

subject: MARTIUS
source_text: LARTIUS. So the good horse is mine. MARTIUS. I’ll buy him of you. LARTIUS. No, I’ll nor sell nor give him. Lend you him I will For half a hundred years.—Summon the town.

subject: MARTIUS
source_text: Tullus Aufidius, is he within your walls? FIRST SENATOR. No, nor a man that fears you less than he:

subject: Battle at Corioles
source_text: Enter two Senators with others on the walls of Corioles.

subject: Tullus Aufidius
source_text: Hark you, far off! There is Aufidius. List what work he makes Amongst your cloven army.

subject: MARTIUS
source_text: O, they are at it! LARTIUS. Their noise be our instruction.—Ladders, ho! Enter the Army of the Volsces as through the city gates. MARTIUS. They fear us not but issue forth their city.— Now put your shields before your hearts, and fight With hearts more proof than shields.—Advance, brave Titus. They do disdain us much beyond our thoughts, Which makes me sweat with wrath.—Come on, my fellows! He that retires, I’ll take him for a Volsce, And he shall feel mine edge.

subject: LARTIUS
source_text: LARTIUS. Their noise be our instruction.—Ladders, ho!

subject: Army of the Volsces
source_text: Enter the Army of the Volsces as through the city gates. MARTIUS. They fear us not but issue forth their city.—

subject: Roman soldiers
source_text: Now put your shields before your hearts, and fight With hearts more proof than shields.

subject: MARTIUS
source_text: He that retires, I’ll take him for a Volsce, And he shall feel mine edge.

source_text: The Romans are beat back to their trenches.

subject: Roman soldiers
source_text: The Romans are beat back to their trenches. They exit, with the Volsces following

subject: MARTIUS
source_text: MARTIUS. I’ll buy him of you.

subject: THE TRAGEDY OF CORIOLANUS
source_text: You shames of Rome! You herd of—Boils and plagues Plaster you o’er

subject: Martius
source_text: Mark me, and do the like. [_Martius follows the fleeing Volsces through the gates, and is shut in._]

subject: The assault on Corioles
source_text: [_Another alarum. The Volsces re-enter and are driven back to the gates of Corioles, which open to admit them._]

subject: Martius
source_text: Martius follows the fleeing Volsces through the gates, and is shut in.

subject: Titus Lartius
source_text: LARTIUS. What is become of Martius? ALL. Slain, sir, doubtless. ... LARTIUS. O noble fellow, Who sensibly outdares his senseless sword, And when it bows, stand’st up! Thou art left, Martius.

subject: Titus Lartius
source_text: LARTIUS. O, ’tis Martius! Let’s fetch him off or make remain alike. [_They fight, and all enter the city._]

subject: The rescue of Martius
source_text: He is himself alone, To answer all the city. ... [_They fight, and all enter the city._]

subject: Martius
source_text: O noble fellow, Who sensibly outdares his senseless sword, And when it bows, stand’st up!

source_text: SCENE V. Within Corioles. A street. Enter certain Romans, with spoils.

subject: Martius
source_text: There is the man of my soul’s hate, Aufidius,

subject: Aufidius
source_text: Aufidius, Piercing our Romans.

subject: Titus
source_text: Then, valiant Titus, take Convenient numbers to make good the city,

subject: Martius
source_text: Whilst I, with those that have the spirit, will haste To help Cominius.

subject: Martius
source_text: To Aufidius thus I will appear and fight.

subject: Martius
source_text: MARTIUS. Thy friend no less Than those she placeth highest! So farewell. LARTIUS. Thou worthiest Martius!

subject: Lartius
source_text: Go sound thy trumpet in the marketplace.

subject: Lartius
source_text: Go sound thy trumpet in the marketplace.

subject: Cominius
source_text: Breathe you, my friends. Well fought! We are come off Like Romans, neither foolish in our stands Nor cowardly in retire.

subject: Lartius
source_text: The citizens of Corioles have issued, And given to Lartius and to Martius battle.

subject: Martius
source_text: The citizens of Corioles have issued, And given to Lartius and to Martius battle.

subject: battle with citizens of Corioles
source_text: The citizens of Corioles have issued, And given to Lartius and to Martius battle.

subject: Messenger
source_text: Enter a Messenger. Thy news? MESSENGER. The citizens of Corioles have issued, And given to Lartius and to Martius battle.

subject: MESSENGER
source_text: Above an hour, my lord.

subject: Spies of the Volsces
source_text: Spies of the Volsces Held me in chase

subject: MESSENGER
source_text: Spies of the Volsces Held me in chase, that I was forced to wheel Three or four miles about

subject: Chase of the Messenger
source_text: Spies of the Volsces Held me in chase, that I was forced to wheel Three or four miles about; else had I, sir, Half an hour since brought my report.

subject: COMINIUS
source_text: The shepherd knows not thunder from a tabor More than I know the sound of Martius’ tongue From every meaner man.

subject: MARTIUS
source_text: Enter Martius, bloody. ... Ay, if you come not in the blood of others, But mantled in your own.

subject: Titus Lartius
source_text: Holding Corioles in the name of Rome

subject: Holding of Corioles
source_text: Holding Corioles in the name of Rome

subject: our gentlemen
source_text: But for our gentlemen, The common file—a plague! Tribunes for them!— The mouse ne’er shunned the cat as they did budge From rascals worse than they.

subject: COMINIUS
source_text: Martius, we have at disadvantage fought, And did retire to win our purpose.

subject: The battle
source_text: Where is the enemy? Are you lords o’ th’ field? If not, why cease you till you are so?

subject: Caius Martius
source_text: that you directly set me Against Aufidius and his Antiates

subject: Caius Martius
source_text: that you directly set me Against Aufidius and his Antiates

subject: Aufidius
source_text: Their bands i’ th’ vaward are the Antiates, Of their best trust; o’er them Aufidius, Their very heart of hope.

subject: Caius Martius
source_text: By all the battles wherein we have fought, By th’ blood we have shed together, by th’ vows we have made To endure friends

subject: Caius Martius
source_text: And follow Martius. [_He waves his sword._]

subject: Soldiers
source_text: [_They all shout and wave their swords, take him up in their arms, and cast up their caps._]

subject: Aufidius
source_text: None of you but is Able to bear against the great Aufidius A shield as hard as his.

source_text: Alarum, as in battle. Enter Martius and Aufidius at several doors.

subject: Martius
source_text: I’ll fight with none but thee, for I do hate thee Worse than a promise-breaker.

subject: Aufidius
source_text: We hate alike. Not Afric owns a serpent I abhor More than thy fame and envy.

subject: Martius
source_text: Alone I fought in your Corioles’ walls, And made what work I pleased.

subject: Fighting in Corioles' walls
source_text: Alone I fought in your Corioles’ walls,

subject: Martius
source_text: If I should tell thee o’er this thy day’s work, Thou’t not believe thy deeds.

subject: Titus Lartius
source_text: Enter Titus Lartius with his power, from the pursuit.

subject: Martius's mother
source_text: My mother, Who has a charter to extol her blood, When she does praise me grieves me.

subject: Martius
source_text: I thank you, general,

subject: Cominius
source_text: Of all the horses— Whereof we have ta’en good and good store—of all The treasure in this field achieved and city, We render you the tenth, to be ta’en forth Before the common distribution At your only choice.

subject: Martius
source_text: I do refuse it; And stand upon my common part with those That have beheld the doing.

subject: offering a reward to Martius
source_text: We render you the tenth, to be ta’en forth Before the common distribution At your only choice. MARTIUS. I thank you, general, But cannot make my heart consent to take A bribe to pay my sword. I do refuse it;

subject: refusing a reward
source_text: I do refuse it; And stand upon my common part with those That have beheld the doing. [_A long flourish. They all cry “Martius, Martius!” and cast up their caps and lances. Cominius and Lartius stand bare._]

subject: Cominius
source_text: [_A long flourish. They all cry “Martius, Martius!” and cast up their caps and lances. Cominius and Lartius stand bare._]

source_text: [_A long flourish. They all cry “Martius, Martius!” and cast up their caps and lances. Cominius and Lartius stand bare._]

subject: Martius's fighting
source_text: I have done As you have done—that’s what I can; Induced as you have been—that’s for my country.

subject: Martius
source_text: I have done As you have done—that’s what I can; Induced as you have been—that’s for my country.

subject: Caius Martius
source_text: Therefore be it known, As to us to all the world, that Caius Martius Wears this war’s garland, in token of the which My noble steed, known to the camp, I give him, With all his trim belonging.

subject: Cominius
source_text: My noble steed, known to the camp, I give him, With all his trim belonging.

subject: Caius Martius
source_text: My noble steed, known to the camp, I give him, With all his trim belonging.

subject: Cominius
source_text: And from this time, For what he did before Corioles, call him, With all th’ applause and clamour of the host, Caius Martius Coriolanus!

subject: Caius Martius
source_text: And from this time, For what he did before Corioles, call him, With all th’ applause and clamour of the host, Caius Martius Coriolanus!

subject: Coriolanus
source_text: I mean to stride your steed and at all times To undercrest your good addition To th’ fairness of my power.

subject: Cominius
source_text: So, to our tent, Where, ere we do repose us, we will write To Rome of our success.

subject: Titus Lartius
source_text: You, Titus Lartius, Must to Corioles back.

subject: Coriolanus
source_text: I sometime lay here in Corioles At a poor man’s house; he used me kindly.

subject: Coriolanus
source_text: I sometime lay here in Corioles At a poor man’s house; he used me kindly.

subject: Coriolanus
source_text: But then Aufidius was within my view, And wrath o’erwhelmed my pity.

subject: Coriolanus
source_text: I request you To give my poor host freedom.

subject: Cominius
source_text: I request you To give my poor host freedom. COMINIUS. O, well begged!

subject: Titus Lartius
source_text: Deliver him, Titus.

subject: Cominius
source_text: COMINIUS. Go we to our tent.

subject: the people
source_text: Not according to the prayer of the people, for they love not Martius.

subject: plebeians
source_text: Ay, to devour him, as the hungry plebeians would the noble Martius.

subject: Brutus
source_text: BRUTUS. He’s a lamb indeed, that baas like a bear.

subject: Sicinius
source_text: BRUTUS. He’s poor in no one fault, but stored with all. SICINIUS. Especially in pride.

subject: Menenius
source_text: MENENIUS. He’s a bear indeed, that lives like a lamb.

subject: Menenius
source_text: Why, then you should discover a brace of unmeriting, proud, violent, testy magistrates, alias fools, as any in Rome.

subject: Menenius
source_text: Why, then you should discover a brace of unmeriting, proud, violent, testy magistrates, alias fools, as any in Rome.

subject: Brutus
source_text: BOTH TRIBUNES. Well, sir.

subject: Brutus
source_text: You blame Martius for being proud. BRUTUS. We do it not alone, sir.

subject: Sicinius
source_text: You blame Martius for being proud.

subject: Blaming Martius for being proud
source_text: You blame Martius for being proud.

subject: Censuring the tribunes
source_text: Do you two know how you are censured here in the city, I mean of us o’ th’ right-hand file, do you?

subject: Censuring the tribunes
source_text: Why, then you should discover a brace of unmeriting, proud, violent, testy magistrates, alias fools, as any in Rome.

subject: THE TRAGEDY OF CORIOLANUS
source_text: Why, then you should discover a brace of unmeriting, proud, violent, testy magistrates, alias fools, as any in Rome.

subject: Brutus
source_text: BRUTUS. Good or bad?

subject: Menenius
source_text: MENENIUS. Not according to the prayer of the people, for they love not Martius.

subject: Sicinius
source_text: SICINIUS. Nature teaches beasts to know their friends.

subject: Martius
source_text: MENENIUS. Not according to the prayer of the people, for they love not Martius.

subject: the augurer
source_text: The augurer tells me we shall have news tonight.

subject: Menenius
source_text: I am known to be a humorous patrician and one that loves a cup of hot wine with not a drop of allaying Tiber in’t;

subject: Menenius
source_text: Meeting two such wealsmen as you are—I cannot call you Lycurguses—if the drink you give me touch my palate adversely, I make a crooked face at it.

subject: Brutus
source_text: BRUTUS. Come, sir, come; we know you well enough.

subject: Brutus
object: hearing a cause between an orange-wife and a faucet-seller
source_text: You wear out a good wholesome forenoon in hearing a cause between an orange-wife and a faucet-seller, and then rejourn the controversy of threepence to a second day of audience.

subject: Brutus
source_text: When you are hearing a matter between party and party, if you chance to be pinched with the colic, you make faces like mummers, set up the bloody flag against all patience, and, in roaring for a chamber pot, dismiss the controversy bleeding, the more entangled by your hearing.

subject: being a bencher
source_text: You are well understood to be a perfecter giber for the table than a necessary bencher in the Capitol.

subject: Volumnia
source_text: Honourable Menenius, my boy Martius approaches.

subject: Virgilia
source_text: Look, here’s a letter from him. The state hath another, his wife another, and I think there’s one at home for you.

subject: Martius
source_text: Has he disciplined Aufidius soundly?

subject: Menenius
source_text: VOLUMNIA. Ay, worthy Menenius, and with most prosperous approbation. ... and I think there’s one at home for you.

subject: Martius
source_text: Honourable Menenius, my boy Martius approaches. ... MENENIUS. Ha? Martius coming home?

subject: Martius
source_text: O, he is wounded, I thank the gods for’t.

subject: Martius

subject: Coriolanus
source_text: Therefore, for Coriolanus neither to care whether they love or hate him manifests the true knowledge he has in their disposition and, out of his noble carelessness, lets them plainly see’t.

subject: First Officer
source_text: FIRST OFFICER. If he did not care whether he had their love or no, he waved indifferently ’twixt doing them neither good nor harm; but he seeks their hate with greater devotion than they can render it him and leaves nothing undone that may fully discover him their opposite.

subject: Second Officer
source_text: SECOND OFFICER. He hath deserved worthily of his country, and his ascent is not by such easy degrees as those who, having been supple and courteous to the people, bonnetted, without any further deed to have them at all into their estimation and report; but he hath so planted his honours in their eyes and his actions in their hearts that for their tongues to be silent and not confess so much were a kind of ingrateful injury.

subject: The senate’s courtesy

Let deeds express What’s like to be their words: “We did request it; We are the greater poll, and in true fear They gave us our demands.” Thus we debase The nature of our seats and make the rabble Call our cares fears, which will in time Break ope the locks o’ th’ Senate and bring in The crows to peck the eagles.

subject: Farewell of Coriolanus
source_text:
SCENE I. Rome. Before a gate of the city





Enter Coriolanus, Volumnia, Virgilia, Menenius, Cominius with the young
nobility of Rome. CORIOLANUS. Come, leave your tears. A brief farewell.

subject: Volumnia
source_text: Farewell, my wife, my mother.

subject: Virgilia
source_text: Farewell, my wife, my mother.

subject: Menenius
source_text: My sometime general, I have seen thee stern, and thou hast oft beheld Heart-hard’ning spectacles.

subject: Volumnia
source_text: My mother, you wot well... VOLUMNIA. My first son, Whither wilt thou go?

subject: Virgilia
source_text: Come, my sweet wife, my dearest mother, and My friends of noble touch.

subject: Cominius
source_text: Take good Cominius With thee awhile. ... COMINIUS. I’ll follow thee a month, devise with thee Where thou shalt rest

subject: Menenius
source_text: My friends of noble touch. ... MENENIUS. ... If I could shake off but one seven years From these old arms and legs, by the good gods, I’d with thee every foot.

subject: Sicinius
source_text: Say their great enemy is gone, and they Stand in their ancient strength.

subject: Brutus
source_text: BRUTUS. Now we have shown our power... SICINIUS. Bid them home. Say their great enemy is gone...

subject: Virgilia
source_text: Come, my sweet wife, my dearest mother, and My friends of noble touch. When I am forth, Bid me farewell, and smile.

subject: Cominius
source_text: COMINIUS. I’ll follow thee a month, devise with thee Where thou shalt rest, that thou mayst hear of us And we of thee

subject: Menenius
source_text: MENENIUS. That’s worthily As any ear can hear. Come, let’s not weep. If I could shake off but one seven years From these old arms and legs, by the good gods, I’d with thee every foot.

subject: Nicanor
source_text: I know you well, sir, and you know me. Your name I think is Adrian. VOLSCE. It is so, sir. Truly, I have forgot you. ROMAN. I am a Roman, and my services are, as you are, against ’em. Know you me yet? VOLSCE. Nicanor, no? ROMAN. The same, sir.

subject: strange insurrections

subject: ROMAN
source_text: I am most fortunate thus accidentally to encounter you. You have ended my business, and I will merrily accompany you home.

subject: THE TRAGEDY OF CORIOLANUS
source_text: I shall between this and supper tell you most strange things from Rome, all tending to the good of their adversaries.

source_text: Enter Coriolanus in mean apparel, disguised and muffled. CORIOLANUS. A goodly city is this Antium.

source_text: A goodly city is this Antium. City, ’Tis I that made thy widows. Many an heir Of these fair edifices ’fore my wars Have I heard groan and drop.

subject: friends becoming enemies
source_text: Friends now fast sworn, Whose double bosoms seems to wear one heart... shall within this hour, On a dissension of a doit, break out To bitterest enmity

My birthplace hate I, and my love’s upon

SECOND SERVINGMAN. Whence are you, sir?—Has the porter his eyes in his head, that he gives
entrance to such companions?—Pray, get you out.

subject: Coriolanus
source_text:
CORIOLANUS. I have deserved no better entertainment
In being Coriolanus.

here.

subject: Coriolanus
source_text: My name is Caius Martius, who hath done To thee particularly and to all the Volsces Great hurt and mischief; thereto witness may My surname Coriolanus.

source_text: Then if thou hast A heart of wreak in thee, that wilt revenge Thine own particular wrongs and stop those maims Of shame seen through thy country, speed thee straight And make my misery serve thy turn. So use it That my revengeful services may prove As benefits to thee

source_text: Well, and say that Martius Return me, as Cominius is returned, unheard, What then?

source_text: Yet your good will Must have that thanks from Rome after the measure As you intended well.

subject: Cominius's appeal to Martius
source_text: But as a discontented friend, Grief-shot with his unkindness? Say’t be so?

subject: Plea to Menenius for aid
source_text: But sure, if you Would be your country’s pleader, your good tongue, More than the instant army we can make, Might stop our countryman.

subject: Second Watch

source_text: Therefore back to Rome and prepare for your execution.

source_text: Thou art my warrior; I holp to frame thee.

subject: VALERIA
source_text: The noble sister of Publicola, The moon of Rome, chaste as the icicle That’s curdied by the frost from purest snow And hangs on Dian’s temple!—Dear Valeria.

subject: CORIOLANUS
source_text: CORIOLANUS. That’s my brave boy!

subject: CORIOLANUS
source_text: [_They kiss._] O, a kiss Long as my exile, sweet as my revenge! Now, by the jealous queen of heaven, that kiss I carried from thee, dear, and my true lip Hath virgined it e’er since.

subject: CORIOLANUS
source_text: O, a kiss Long as my exile, sweet as my revenge!

subject: CORIOLANUS

subject: young Martius

source_text: If I cannot persuade thee Rather to show a noble grace to both parts Than seek the end of one, thou shalt no sooner March to assault thy country than to tread— Trust to’t, thou shalt not—on thy mother’s womb That brought thee to this world.

source_text: Thou know’st, great son, The end of war’s uncertain... Speak to me, son... There’s no man in the world More bound to’s mother...

subject: Volumnia
source_text: No, our suit Is that you reconcile them, while the Volsces May say “This mercy we have showed,” the Romans “This we received,” and each in either side Give the all-hail to thee and cry, “Be blessed For making up this peace!”

source_text: That, if thou conquer Rome, the benefit Which thou shalt thereby reap is such a name Whose repetition will be dogged with curses,

subject: Ladies
source_text: Down, ladies! Let us shame him with our knees. To his surname Coriolanus ’longs more pride Than pity to our prayers. Down! An end. [_They kneel._]

subject: Boy
source_text: This boy that cannot tell what he would have, But kneels and holds up hands for fellowship, Does reason our petition with more strength Than thou hast to deny’t.

subject: Mother
source_text: You have won a happy victory to Rome,

subject: Coriolanus

subject: Aufidius

subject: The gods

subject: The mother prevailing over Coriolanus
source_text: Most dangerously you have with him prevailed, If not most mortal to him. But let it come.— Aufidius, though I cannot make true wars, I’ll frame convenient peace.

subject: POSTHUMUS
source_text: Your ring may be stol’n too. So your brace of unprizable estimations, the one is but frail and the other casual; a cunning thief, or a that-way-accomplish’d courtier, would hazard the winning both of first and last. POSTHUMUS. Your Italy contains none so accomplish’d a courtier to convince the honour of my mistress, if in the holding or loss of that you term her frail. I do nothing doubt you have store of thieves; notwithstanding, I fear not my ring.

subject: IACHIMO
source_text: I dare thereupon pawn the moiety of my estate to your ring, which, in my opinion, o’ervalues it something. But I make my wager rather against your confidence than her reputation; and, to bar your offence herein too, I durst attempt it against any lady in the world.

subject: POSTHUMUS
source_text: POSTHUMUS. You are a great deal abus’d in too bold a persuasion, and I doubt not you sustain what y’are worthy of by your attempt.

subject: PHILARIO

I will try the forces
Of these thy compounds on such creatures as
We count not worth the hanging (but none human)
To try the vigour of them, and apply
Allayments to their act, and by them gather
Their several virtues and effects.

subject: IACHIMO
source_text: IMOGEN. You do seem to know Something of me, or what concerns me; pray you, Since doubting things go ill often hurts more Than to be sure they do; for certainties Either are past remedies, or, timely knowing, The remedy then born—discover to me What both you spur and stop.

subject: IACHIMO
source_text: IMOGEN. What do you pity, sir? IACHIMO. Two creatures heartily.

subject: IMOGEN
source_text: I pray you, sir, Deliver with more openness your answers To my demands. Why do you pity me?

subject: IACHIMO
source_text: IACHIMO. And himself. Not I Inclin’d to this intelligence pronounce The beggary of his change; but ’tis your graces That from my mutest conscience to my tongue Charms this report out.

subject: Pisanio
source_text: IMOGEN. What ho, Pisanio!

subject: IACHIMO
source_text: IACHIMO. O dearest soul, your cause doth strike my heart With pity that doth make me sick!

subject: IMOGEN
source_text: IMOGEN. What ho, Pisanio!

subject: IACHIMO
source_text: I dedicate myself to your sweet pleasure, More noble than that runagate to your bed, And will continue fast to your affection, Still close as sure.

subject: IMOGEN
source_text: IMOGEN. Away! I do condemn mine ears that have So long attended thee.

subject: IMOGEN
source_text: IMOGEN. Reveng’d? How should I be reveng’d? If this be true, (As I have such a heart that both mine ears Must not in haste abuse) if it be true, How should I be reveng’d?

subject: Iachimo's report
source_text: Be reveng’d; Or she that bore you was no queen, and you Recoil from your great stock. IMOGEN. Reveng’d? How should I be reveng’d?

subject: Imogen

subject: Imogen
source_text: The credit that thy lady hath of thee Deserves thy trust, and thy most perfect goodness Her assur’d credit. Blessed live you long, A lady to the worthiest sir that ever Country call’d his! and you his mistress, only For the most worthiest fit!

subject: Iachimo
source_text: The love I bear him Made me to fan you thus; but the gods made you, Unlike all others, chaffless.

subject: Leonatus Posthumus

subject: Letter from Leonatus Posthumus
source_text: [_Reads._] Justice and your father’s wrath, should he take me in his dominion, could not be so cruel to me as you, O the dearest of creatures, would even renew me with your eyes. Take notice that I am in Cambria, at Milford Haven. What your own love will out of this advise you, follow. So he wishes you all happiness that remains loyal to his vow, and your increasing in love. LEONATUS POSTHUMUS.

subject: BELARIUS
source_text: BELARIUS. How you speak! Did you but know the city’s usuries, And felt them knowingly; the art o’ th’ court, As hard to leave as keep, whose top to climb Is certain falling, or so slipp’ry that The fear’s as bad as falling; the toil o’ th’ war, A pain that only seems to seek out danger I’ th’ name of fame and honour, which dies i’ th’ search, And hath as oft a sland’rous epitaph As record of fair act; nay, many times, Doth ill deserve by doing well; what’s worse, Must curtsy at the censure. O, boys, this story The world may read in me; my body’s mark’d With Roman swords, and my report was once First with the best of note. Cymbeline lov’d me; And when a soldier was the theme, my name Was not far off. Then was I as a tree Whose boughs did bend with fruit. But in one night A storm, or robbery, call it what you will, Shook down my mellow hangings, nay, my leaves, And left me bare to weather. GUIDERIUS. Uncertain favour!

subject: Cymbeline

subject: Imogen
source_text: Yes, sir, to Milford Haven. Which is the way? I thank you. By yond bush? Pray, how far thither? ’Ods pittikins! can it be six mile yet? I have gone all night.

subject: Imogen

I know the shape of’s leg; this is his hand, His foot Mercurial, his Martial thigh, The brawns of Hercules; but his Jovial face— Murder in heaven! How! ’Tis gone. Pisanio, All curses madded Hecuba gave the Greeks, And mine to boot, be darted on thee! Thou, Conspir’d with that irregulous devil, Cloten, Hath here cut off my lord.

subject: Hamlet's 'To be, or not to be' soliloquy
source_text: To die—to sleep, No more; and by a sleep to say we end The heart-ache, and the thousand natural shocks That flesh is heir to: ’tis a consummation Devoutly to be wish’d. To die, to sleep. To sleep, perchance to dream—ay, there’s the rub, For in that sleep of death what dreams may come, When we have shuffled off this mortal coil, Must give us pause.

subject: Hamlet
source_text: ‘Horatio, when thou shalt have overlooked this, give these fellows some means to the King. They have letters for him. Ere we were two days old at sea, a pirate of very warlike appointment gave us chase. Finding ourselves too slow of sail, we put on a compelled valour, and in the grapple I boarded them. On the instant they got clear of our ship, so I alone became their prisoner. They have dealt with me like thieves of mercy. But they knew what they did; I am to do a good turn for them. Let the King have the letters I have sent, and repair thou to me with as much haste as thou wouldst fly death. I have words to speak in thine ear will make thee dumb; yet are they much too light for the bore of the matter. These good fellows will bring thee where I am. Rosencrantz and Guildenstern hold their course for England: of them I have much to tell thee. Farewell. He that thou knowest thine, HAMLET.’

subject: upholding idleness
source_text: Yet herein will I imitate the sun, Who doth permit the base contagious clouds To smother up his beauty from the world, That, when he please again to be himself, Being wanted, he may be more wonder’d at, By breaking through the foul and ugly mists Of vapours that did seem to strangle him.

subject: having his pocket picked
source_text: I fell asleep here, behind the arras, and had my pocket picked. ... What didst thou lose, Jack? ... three or four bonds of forty pound apiece and a seal-ring of my grandfather’s.

subject: Falstaff
source_text: What didst thou lose, Jack? FALSTAFF. Wilt thou believe me, Hal, three or four bonds of forty pound apiece and a seal-ring of my grandfather’s.

subject: Falstaff
source_text: How? The Prince is a Jack, a sneak-up. ’Sblood, an he were here, I would cudgel him like a dog if he would say so.

subject: Prince Henry

subject: Hostess

source_text: He writes me here, that inward sickness—

subject: Worcester

subject: Douglas

subject: The Earl
source_text: But yet I would your father had been here.

subject: WORCESTER
source_text: The quality and hair of our attempt Brooks no division.

subject: DOUGLAS
source_text: Faith, and so we should, where now remains A sweet reversion. We may boldly spend Upon the hope of what is to come in.

subject: The absence of your father
object: a kind of question in our cause
source_text: This absence of your father’s draws a curtain That shows the ignorant a kind of fear Before not dreamt of. ... And think how such an apprehension May turn the tide of fearful faction, And breed a kind of question in our cause.

subject: FALSTAFF
source_text: Hal, if thou see me down in the battle and bestride me, so; ’tis a point of friendship.

subject: FALSTAFF
source_text: Hal, if thou see me down in the battle and bestride me, so; ’tis a point of friendship.

subject: Prince of Wales
source_text: Hal, if thou see me down in the battle and bestride me, so; ’tis a point of friendship.

subject: the battle
source_text: Well, ’tis no matter, honour pricks me on. Yea, but how if honour prick me off when I come on? How then? Can honor set to a leg? No. Or an arm? No. Or take away the grief of a wound? No. Honour hath no skill in surgery then? No. What is honour? A word. What is in that word, “honour”? What is that “honour”? Air.

subject: Catechism on honour
source_text: Honour is a mere scutcheon. And so ends my catechism.

subject: Worcester
source_text: O no, my nephew must not know, Sir Richard, The liberal and kind offer of the King.

subject: Worcester's speech on the King's untrustworthiness
source_text: He will suspect us still, and find a time To punish this offence in other faults. Suspicion all our lives shall be stuck full of eyes,

subject: Worcester's speech on the King's untrustworthiness
source_text: For treason is but trusted like the fox, Who, ne’er so tame, so cherish’d and lock’d up, Will have a wild trick of his ancestors.

subject: Hotspur
source_text: All his offences live upon my head And on his father’s.

subject: Worcester
source_text: Therefore, good cousin, let not Harry know In any case the offer of the King.

source_text: O, would the quarrel lay upon our heads, And that no man might draw short breath today But I and Harry Monmouth!

subject: Hotspur

source_text: But be he as he will, yet once ere night I will embrace him with a soldier’s arm, That he shall shrink under my courtesy.

subject: Hotspur
source_text: And here draw I A sword whose temper I intend to stain With the best blood that I can meet withal In the adventure of this perilous day.

subject: Douglas
source_text: DOUGLAS. A fool go with thy soul, whither it goes! A borrow’d title hast thou bought too dear. Why didst thou tell me that thou wert a king?

subject: Sir Walter Blunt
source_text: A gallant knight he was, his name was Blunt, ... DOUGLAS. A fool go with thy soul, whither it goes! ... FALSTAFF. ... Sir Walter Blunt. There’s honour for you.

subject: Lancaster's departure
source_text: LANCASTER. We breathe too long. Come, cousin Westmoreland, Our duty this way lies. For God’s sake, come. [_Exeunt Lancaster and Westmoreland._]

subject: Prince Henry's refusal to retreat
source_text: And God forbid a shallow scratch should drive The Prince of Wales from such a field as this, Where stain’d nobility lies trodden on, And rebels’ arms triumph in massacres!

subject: Douglas
source_text: Enter Douglas. DOUGLAS. Another king! They grow like Hydra’s heads. I am the Douglas, fatal to all those That wear those colours on them. What art thou That counterfeit’st the person of a king?

subject: King Henry
source_text: I will assay thee, and defend thyself.
